caveats: a: No public wage-weighted task inventory specific to NAICS 311225 was found.
a: Equipment-vendor materials may describe newer systems than those installed at LMM plants.
rho: This is bounded implementation judgment rather than a measured adoption rate.
rho: Packaged specialty producers and bulk commodity refiners may have very different task mixes and integration budgets.
e: The frozen estimate of 28 LMM firms is margin-bridged rather than observed.
e: NAICS combines bulk refining, blending, shortenings, margarine, packaged oils, and specialty products with different transferability.
s5: Gallup covers all employer businesses, not this NAICS or EBITDA band.
s5: Plans include gifts and may never close; internal reorganizations would not qualify.
q: The source is an integrated global processor, not a pure-play LMM purchased-oil refiner.
q: No public study directly measures retention of AI-enabled gross benefits.
d5: Soybean oil and biofuel evidence covers only part of the heterogeneous service basket.
d5: Federal and state clean-fuel policy changes can sharply alter industrial oil demand.
o: This is a structural estimate rather than a measured five-year operator-retention share.
o: Quantity growth or decline is captured in d5 rather than counted again here.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring B2B and private-label sales by bulk shipment or packaged product, with revenue tied to input-oil prices, refining and blending fees, formulation, quality specifications, service reliability, and in some cases branded or specialty-product premiums.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal units, refining within oilseed-crushing or wet-corn-milling establishments, animal rendering and stand-alone animal-fat refining, shell entities, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Independent merchant firms refining or blending purchased vegetable, oilseed, tree-nut, and eligible mixed animal-vegetable fats; manufacturing shortening, margarine, nondairy butter, cooking oils, sprays, and specification blends for repeat external food, foodservice, retail, biofuel, and industrial customers.

a.bridge: BLS food-processing equipment duties and edible-oil process descriptions proxy the 311225 wage and task mix. I map production records, process surveillance, quality documentation, laboratory review, maintenance triage, scheduling, formulation support, procurement, and customer administration to AI exposure, while excluding most material handling, sanitation, maintenance, packaging, and safety-critical operation. The proxy may overstate remaining opportunity because refining systems already integrate controlled stages and automated instruments are available for key oil-quality measures.
caveats: No public wage-weighted task inventory specific to NAICS 311225 was found.
Equipment-vendor materials may describe newer systems than those installed at LMM plants.
a.change_evidence: A 311225-specific OEWS wage and staffing table plus time-and-motion studies across control room, laboratory, maintenance, blending, packaging, logistics, sales, and administration that identify already automated tasks.
a.rationale: Refining and blending combine information-rich monitoring and quality work with continuous physical operations. AI can assist exception detection, records, planning, maintenance, formulation, and specification workflows, but core degumming, neutralization, bleaching, deodorization, blending, cleaning, and packaging require equipment and accountable operators.

d5.bridge: USDA reports record U.S. soybean crush and growing soybean-oil use tied to biomass-based diesel, while EIA forecasts 2026 renewable-diesel production up 24% and biodiesel up 41%, with further 2027 increases. I translate upstream oil supply and downstream biofuel growth into a five-year constant-quality refining-and-blending demand range, tempered because NAICS 311225 also serves mature food markets and because biofuel policies, feedstock substitution, imports, and integrated refining can redirect volume away from merchant firms. The near-term biofuel figures may overstate durable five-year growth.
caveats: Soybean oil and biofuel evidence covers only part of the heterogeneous service basket.
Federal and state clean-fuel policy changes can sharply alter industrial oil demand.
d5.change_evidence: USDA Oil Crops tables by food and industrial use, EIA and EPA biofuel volumes and feedstocks through 2031, packaged-oil and shortening shipment data, announced refinery capacity, import shares, and customer formulation forecasts.
d5.rationale: Food use provides a broad recurring base, while renewable diesel and biodiesel add growth in refined and blended feedstocks. Specialty oils, nondairy fats, and food formulations can help, but mature cooking-oil demand and customer integration keep the center below extrapolation of current biofuel growth rates.

caveats: The frozen estimate of 28 LMM firms is margin-bridged rather than observed.
NAICS combines bulk refining, blending, shortenings, margarine, packaged oils, and specialty products with different transferability.
e.change_evidence: An establishment-to-firm map for 311225 with ownership, EBITDA, merchant versus captive output, product and customer mix, certifications, private-label or branded share, shared infrastructure, environmental reserves, and owner dependence.
e.rationale: A standalone merchant refiner with repeat external customers, transferable formulas and certifications, compliant assets, and separable procurement and logistics can fit the lens. Eligibility falls for captive or integrated facilities, brand-dependent businesses outside the operating lens, shared plants, customer or supplier concentration, weak traceability, environmental liabilities, and plants whose throughput economics do not support standalone control ownership.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 311225 ($704,951,474) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 311225 ($19,904,140,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1525 (margins.json[31]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Food Processing EBITDA/Sales 15.25% (dominant subsector of NAICS 31)); revenue/employee $2,317,939 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 311225); class <5: 34 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,417,941 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $216,236 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 9 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,016,778 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $612,559 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 14 firms, avg revenue/firm $29,022,214 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,425,888 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 14 firms, avg revenue/firm $43,638,929 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,654,937 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 8 firms, avg revenue/firm $408,368,250 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $62,276,158 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 16 firms, avg revenue/firm $970,973,562 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $148,073,468 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 28

caveats: This is a structural estimate rather than a measured five-year operator-retention share.
Quantity growth or decline is captured in d5 rather than counted again here.
o.change_evidence: Customer make-versus-buy surveys, announced captive refinery capacity, import penetration, toll-refining trends, alternative-fat qualification studies, and evidence of direct sourcing that bypasses independent refiners.
o.rationale: Software cannot degum, neutralize, dewax, bleach, deodorize, blend, test, package, and ship physical fats and oils. AI may reduce labor and improve control, but an accountable operator remains necessary for quality and food safety. Residual displacement reflects customer self-supply, vertical integration, imports, direct use of already refined oils, or substitution toward alternative ingredients.

q.bridge: Bunge's filing describes soybean and softseed oil markets as commodity-like, highly competitive, substitutable, and driven by price, quality, distribution, cost, functionality, and technical support. I apply that integrated-producer evidence to purchased-oil refiners: broad cost reductions should pass through in competitive bids and input-linked pricing, while specialty formulations, private label, service, yield, and reliability can retain more. The proxy may understate branded or highly specialized retention and overstate the bargaining power of smaller bulk refiners.
caveats: The source is an integrated global processor, not a pure-play LMM purchased-oil refiner.
No public study directly measures retention of AI-enabled gross benefits.
q.change_evidence: Target contracts and invoice cohorts showing input-price formulas, tolling or conversion fees, renewal cadence, bids, rebates, private-label terms, customer concentration, product-level margins, and realized repricing after productivity changes.
q.rationale: For bulk oils, customers can compare suppliers and substitute feedstocks, while raw-oil prices are visible and economically dominant. Operators retain more where blending know-how, certifications, packaging, technical service, small-batch flexibility, or differentiated shortenings and margarine make the product less interchangeable.

caveats: This is bounded implementation judgment rather than a measured adoption rate.
Packaged specialty producers and bulk commodity refiners may have very different task mixes and integration budgets.
rho.change_evidence: Audited deployments at independent edible-oil refiners reporting baseline hours, validation protocols, model errors, quality excursions, downtime, yield loss, energy use, operator acceptance, and sustained hiring or headcount effects.
rho.rationale: Within five years, document copilots, quality-data review, predictive-maintenance triage, computer vision, production scheduling, formulation assistance, and customer-specification workflows can be deployed around existing controls. Food-safety validation, calibration, cyber risk, legacy integration, allergen and traceability controls, and the cost of a contaminated or off-spec batch constrain implementation.

s5.bridge: Gallup found 22% of U.S. employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. I use a slightly lower center for capital-intensive food manufacturing, where integrated corporate ownership, family succession, environmental diligence, and specialized assets can make a transfer non-qualifying. The broad survey can overstate completed external control sales but omits unplanned strategic transactions.
caveats: Gallup covers all employer businesses, not this NAICS or EBITDA band.
Plans include gifts and may never close; internal reorganizations would not qualify.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, FDA facility records, and state registries used to enumerate every U.S. fats-and-oils refiner control transaction since 2016, plus owner-age and succession interviews for independent LMM operators.
s5.rationale: The five-year survey provides a broad employer-business anchor but does not observe realized 311225 deals. The range reflects uncertainty about owner age, whether transfers are controlling and external, and whether the plant remains a standalone merchant operation.

principal_driver: Recurring physical food demand plus growing biomass-based-diesel feedstock use supports refining throughput.
principal_weakness: Commodity input transparency and substitutable bulk oils constrain commercial retention of broadly available productivity gains.

caveats: The a and rho inputs rely on broad food-processing duties and edible-oil process descriptions rather than a 311225 wage-weighted task inventory or measured AI deployments. Their PROXY and ESTIMATE labels are honest, and downgrading a to ESTIMATE would not change the STRUCTURAL_OUT base tier or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.

The packet marks the lens as non-heterogeneous even though its own caveats distinguish bulk refiners, packaged specialty producers, shortenings, margarine, and private-label operations with different task mixes, pricing, and transferability. Correcting that characterization would improve disclosure but would not itself change the registered base tier or action.

The q base of 0.40 is a judgmental transfer from an integrated global processor's commodity-market disclosures to independent LMM merchant refiners; the filing does not measure retention of AI-enabled benefits or target-level contract pass-through. This is disclosed as a PROXY, and a correction to C would not overcome H as the binding weakest link at the base case.

The d5 range extrapolates near-term soybean-crush and 2026-2027 biofuel forecasts to a five-year, broader fats-and-oils merchant-refining basket. Food-market baseline growth, feedstock shares, policy persistence, and diversion into integrated refining are not directly quantified; the bridge and caveats acknowledge this, and D is not the base-tier constraint.

Firm availability is driven by an estimated 28-firm dataset anchor, an estimated eligibility share, and a broad succession-intent proxy rather than an ownership-and-deal enumeration. This uncertainty is candidly disclosed and is consistent with MODEL_CONDITIONED readiness and VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS.

The o range of 0.95 to 1.00 is a narrow structural estimate without direct make-versus-buy or vertical-integration evidence. The physical-process sources support the direction, but not the precise five-year operator-retention level; correcting it within a plausible range would not change the base tier because H remains below the LOW_PRIORITY weakest-link threshold.

sources_audited.note: The Census page identifies NAICS 311225 and supports the purchased-oil refining, blending, shortening, margarine, and mixed-fat scope, including the exclusions for crushing mills, wet-corn milling, and animal rendering/refining.

BLS supports the broad food-processing duty mix used in the bridge: equipment setup and control, process monitoring, batch records, sanitation, material handling, and quality checks, and it states that automatic weighing and mixing can reduce worker requirements. It is not 311225-specific, as disclosed.

The Oklahoma State extension page supports the stated oil-quality and oxidation risks, quality parameters, refining context, availability of automated analytical instruments, and the voluntary refined-oil FFA threshold of 0.05 percent or less.

Alfa Laval describes the physical degumming, neutralization, dewaxing, bleaching, and deodorization stages and ties them to purity, safety, taste, appearance, yield, and utility use. It is vendor evidence and does not measure installed automation at LMM plants.

Gallup reports that 22 percent of employer-business owners planned to sell, transfer, or take public their business within five years. The article supports the broad anchor, not realized qualifying sales in this NAICS or EBITDA band.
USDA ERS forecasts 2025/26 soybean crush up nearly 3 percent to 2.49 billion bushels and attributes expansion partly to higher soybean-oil use, especially biomass-based diesel feedstock demand.

EIA forecasts 2026 renewable-diesel production up 24 percent and biodiesel production up 41 percent from 2025, with further increases in 2027. This supports the near-term biofuel premise, not a full five-year industry forecast.

Bunge's 2025 Form 10-K supports the commodity character of its principal products, exposure to market prices and substitute products, third-party refined-oil sales, and competition and demand factors relevant to oilseed processing and refining. Its application to independent LMM purchased-oil refiners and to a numeric retention share remains an explicitly disclosed proxy.

run_meta.title: Fats and Oils Refining and Blending

sources.supports: Defines the industry as shortening and margarine manufacturing from purchased fats and oils, refining or blending purchased vegetable, oilseed, and tree-nut oils, and blending purchased animal and vegetable fats.
sources.title: U.S. Census Bureau 2022 NAICS definition: 311225 Fats and Oils Refining and Blending

sources.supports: Food-processing work combines equipment control, process monitoring, production records, sanitation, material handling, and product-quality checks; BLS notes some automatic weighing and mixing reduces worker requirements.
sources.title: BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook: Food Processing Equipment Workers

sources.supports: Explains oxidation and quality risks, standard oil-quality parameters, refining steps, and available automated instruments; reports a voluntary refined-oil FFA standard of at most 0.05%.
sources.title: Oklahoma State University Extension: Edible Oil Quality

sources.supports: Describes controlled degumming, neutralization, dewaxing, bleaching, and deodorization stages needed for purity, safety, taste, appearance, yield, and utility efficiency.
sources.title: Alfa Laval: Edible oil refining process systems

sources.supports: A fall-2024 survey found 22% of U.S. employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, while 52.3% of employer firms had owners age 55 or older.
sources.title: Gallup: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: Forecasts 2025/26 U.S. soybean crush rising nearly 3% to a record 2.49 billion bushels, with higher soybean-oil use especially in biomass-based diesel.
sources.title: USDA ERS: More soybeans being processed in United States to meet rising demand for soybean meal and oil

sources.supports: Forecasts 2026 renewable-diesel production up 24% and biodiesel production up 41% from 2025, with further increases in 2027.
sources.title: U.S. EIA: Higher blending targets drive RIN prices close to record highs

sources.supports: Describes refined oil customers and commodity-like oil markets with competition based on price, quality, product and service offerings, distribution, cost structure, innovation, technical support, and substitution.
sources.title: Bunge Global SA 2025 Form 10-K

title: Fats and Oils Refining and Blending
